summary: Gruber examines the polarizing custom UI of the new Twitter for Mac app, arguing that the Mac Human Interface Guidelines (HIG) are effectively dead — killed by Apple itself when it began experimenting with non-standard UI in its own flagship OS X apps. He frames the debate as a conservative/liberal spectrum: conservatives see non-standard UI as wrong, liberals see standard-only UI as stodgy. While acknowledging thoughtful conservative critiques, he highlights that the most valuable criticism transcends this spectrum — specifically Tim Morgan's observation that Twitter for Mac's sliding animations create a false sense of stacking where none exists. Gruber concludes that iOS-influenced individuality is the new norm for Mac UI design, and resisting this shift is futile.
key_points: The Mac HIG died long ago, killed by Apple itself when it abandoned UI uniformity in its own OS X apps
UI design criticism exists on a conservative/liberal spectrum — 'this is non-standard' vs. 'this isn't good design' are fundamentally different critiques
Twitter for Mac's sliding tab animations are technically excellent but conceptually misleading, creating a false sense of stacking where no stack exists
iOS's sliding animations serve a navigational purpose (sense of place), but Twitter for Mac's tab-switching animations add nothing contextually
Mac UI design is only beginning to absorb iOS influence, and individuality has replaced uniformity as the norm
money_quotes: But the HIG is dead. It died long ago. And it was Apple that killed it.
Apple sets the tone, for better or for worse. Always has, always will.
There's a conservative/liberal sort of fork in UI design, in the sense of traditional/non-traditional.
The conservatives see non-standard custom UI elements as wrong. Liberals see an app built using nothing other than standard system UI elements as boring, old-fashioned, stodgy.

The problem is that once you see that a certain piece of UI criticism is coming from someone at a distant position from yours on the conservative/liberal design spectrum, there's a natural tendency to close your mind and discount everything they say.
They're technically excellent, but conceptually misleading.
Apple's passion for UI uniformity went away with the six-color logo. Change is inevitable. Individuality is the new norm.
key_insight: The most valuable UI criticism transcends the standard-vs-custom debate by identifying design choices that are conceptually misleading regardless of one's tolerance for non-standard interfaces.
